data.title: I Loved Prologis, But It's Time To Say Goodbye (Downgrade)
data.description:
Prologis (PLD) has outperformed many REITs in large part due to the resilience of the logistics growth story. Now, management appears to be targeting a new frontier in data centers. I still have great confidence in the company’s growth trajectory, as warehouses may benefit from secular tailwinds for decades to come. It is mainly the valuation that now concerns me, as the stock does not look compelling relative to alternatives. I am downgrading the stock to a neutral rating.

PLD Stock Price

I last covered PLD in June, where I upgraded the stock to a "Buy" rating after five years of underperformance. The stock has risen around 26% inclusive of dividends, outpacing the broader market.

It may seem hasty to some, but it is time to downgrade.

PLD Stock Key Metrics

PLD is one of the largest logistics real estate investment trusts ("REITs") in the world. This real estate landlord leases warehouses across the world, with the majority of its presence seen in the United States and Europe.

I view warehouses as being one of the highest-quality sub-sectors in the real estate space. Warehouses are mission-critical infrastructure that are necessary to connect the dots between production and sale.

PLD appears to own one of the higher-quality logistics portfolios, as it has focused on areas with higher income density.

Those ideas all sound nice, but my experience in investing (especially with mall REITs) is that just because something looks or sounds nice does not mean that it is a good business. From a numbers perspective, I can state one clear reason why PLD is positioned for long-term growth. Warehouse assets tend to have rent rates well below market, and PLD is no exception, as they estimate that their market spread to in-place rents stands at around 20%.

This can help explain how the company has continued to show stellar growth rates, with same-store NOI jumping 5.2% YoY. The company saw leasing spreads come in at an impressive 29.4%.

These led to core FFO jumping 3.4% YoY to $1.50 per share (excluding net promote income). That growth rate is strong when compared to REIT peers, as growth has been elusive due to the higher interest rate environment.

Management has guided the full year to see up to $5.86 in core FFO per share, representing 6% YoY growth.

PLD ended the quarter with a solid balance sheet with leverage at 5x debt to EBITDA. This isn’t the strongest balance sheet in the sector - Terreno Realty (TRNO) stands out on this metric - but I see little concern with this amount of leverage given both the resilient income stream and the secular growth drivers.

Management has made a meaningful shift in their investment plans. Whereas management has a long and successful track record of developing warehouse assets, they now view data centers (presumably for AI) as the next growth frontier. Management believes that data centers offer higher yields, higher margins, and higher investment potential.

Management calls this a “generational value creation opportunity” that they anticipate will create as much as $25 billion in value over the next decade.

It is understandable why management may be attracted to this opportunity, given that warehouse assets have tended to feature development yields of “only” 6.8%, with the value creation being the spread between that and the projected 5.2% stabilized cap rate.

On the conference call, management implied around $5 billion of potential annual investment in data centers, which might equate to “about 150 basis points of additional growth per annum.” Management believes that “power will be the constraint,” stating that it “won't be capital.” My opinion is that we might want to see results on a long term basis before getting too excited. I wonder if the exuberance of the AI euphoria may be spreading to real estate, with the risk being PLD investing heavily in data center real estate only to see its counterparties go bankrupt prior to completion.

I note that PLD’s balance sheet is in good shape and that my take may prove to be overly cynical, with my intended takeaway mainly being to remain cautious on projected growth assumptions. Regarding their core warehouse portfolio, management noted there are reasons to be optimistic about occupancy growth. Management expects demand to normalize, leading to a “recovery opportunity.” Management noted that they “enter this new phase of the market at a substantially lower market vacancy as compared to prior cycles,” which may allow them more pricing power than in the past.

Is PLD Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

At recent prices, PLD traded at a 3.1% forward dividend yield.

That represents significant compression since I last looked at it, when it traded at a 3.8% yield. I expect the company to sustain solid growth moving forward. I may not be as bullish as others regarding the data center opportunity, but perhaps we might be looking at 3% to 5% growth from the core warehouse assets with the potential for some more if the data center investments work out. If one compares to where the stock has traded just a handful of years ago, the setup might look attractive. However, I was not so bullish on the stock when it traded at such aggressive dividend yields and am even less so today. Net lease REITs like Realty Income (O) are trading weakly at 5% to 6% yields. I also recently wrote a piece regarding the preferred stock of Innovative Industrial Properties (IIPR), which is currently yielding nearly 10%.

PLD still has some upside to my previous target of a 3% yield, but I view the potential upside as insufficient relative to the aforementioned bargains seen elsewhere in the REIT sector. I think there’s an underestimated possibility that PLD does not see its yield compress further. In this scenario, the stock might be priced for just around 8% to 9% returns, but that is assuming that it can achieve the high end of my growth assumptions while experiencing no yield expansion.

PLD Stock Conclusion

PLD is one of the rare true growth names in the REIT sector, and its foray into data centers might help it to retain that title. Investors may want to pay close attention to how the investments in data centers play out, as I think some skepticism may be warranted. It is mainly the valuation that has led to my downgrade. At Best of Breed Growth Stocks, I have preferred net lease REITs and especially IIPR (and its preferred stock). I am downgrading PLD to a "Neutral" rating.

data.title: TTM Technologies: The Tailwinds Will Continue To Push Share Price, But I'll Pass
data.description:
Introduction

TTM Technologies, Inc. (TTMI) had an amazing start to the year and even a better 2025, so I wanted to look into the company’s performance in more detail and see what is still ahead for it. I don’t think the share price appreciation is going to stop right now, given the sentiment and overall exuberance, but from a modelling perspective, there’s very little upside. The company will have to report perfect numbers to continue to see its share price appreciate as it has in the past.

Briefly on Performance

Over the past couple of years, the company saw its sales grow at a somewhat acceptable pace when we look at the 2024 numbers, ranging from around 4%-to 14% in Q4 ’24. However, all of that changed in Q2 ’25, when its sales began to grow at over 20% compared to the same time last year. It seems that the company’s products really began to surge in demand across its two main revenue segments, the Aerospace & Defense, as well as data center computing, which both saw sales increase over 20% y/y.

This makes sense. 2025 has been a massive year for these two segments. Geopolitical conflicts around the globe continued to push budget increases on national security spending, as well as the massive AI race that has been raging on across the hyperscalers. These tailwinds pushed the company’s sales to new highs, surpassing its previous best year of FY17 on a TTM basis.

The most recent quarter showed the company grew its sales by 22% y/y and a quarterly record high in diluted EPS of $0.67. Looking at the company’s margins over the same period, we can see a steady improvement throughout, boosted by more efficient business operations on the back of increased demand for its products, allowing it to leverage its fixed cost structure more effectively. Additionally, a lot of the demand came from high-density interconnect, or HDI PCBs for Gen AI, which naturally come with much higher profitability compared to commoditized boards.

With an increased bottom line comes an increase in other profitability and efficiency metrics; however, they are still quite unappealing. The uptrend that we see is positive, and I do believe that over the next few quarters or even the year, these will double. They may not double if the company increases its Capex spending, but at least the uptrend is going to remain.

Let’s take a look at the company’s financial position. Cash and equivalents stood at around $491m against $913m in long-term debt. $500m of the debt at 4% is due in three years, and that is the closest substantial due date. Interest expense for the recent quarter stood at around $10m, which is easily covered by the company’s EBIT, the mountain of cash, and operating cash flow. Free cash flow for this quarter, however, was only $5m (for the three quarters to date) due to a massive increase in capex. I think this is a positive since the company is preparing for the demand to persist.

Overall, the company’s performance has been very good over the last two years. All metrics are improving and look like they will continue to improve going forward. The decent cash pile supports its ability to continue to expand through establishing more campuses in the U.S. and building out facilities in Penang.

The future looks rather bright, and the management is clearly taking advantage of the current AI craze. Investors have bid up the company’s share price significantly over the past year, which is up almost 300% as of right now, and a whopping 40% in just 5 days, which is a little concerning and suspicious. Someone must have known something about the recent news that came out this morning.

The party is not over

The recent news I am talking about is that the management filed an 8K report regarding the medium-term outlook for the company during the Needham Growth Conference in New York. The filing and the comments say that the company expects to grow its top line by between 15%-20% for the next three full years (‘25-’27) and double earnings in the same period. The news sent the company’s shares up another 20% at one point on Tuesday, and another 5% the following day, as of writing this update. Clearly, this news was very unexpected, which warranted such a reaction from the investing community.

The news is still so fresh that most of the analysts who are covering the company haven’t updated their EPS estimates, apart from Truist, who already has the company’s EPS at around $4.72 by 2027.

So, given the ridiculous demand for everything AI and data center related, the company will continue to ride these tailwinds for a lot longer, at least to 2027. It is no surprise that the hyperscalers are continuing to increase their AI-related CAPEX from already-elevated numbers that they produced for 2025. Companies like Meta (META), Amazon (AMZN), and Microsoft (MSFT) confirmed their plans to increase capex spending related to AI for 2026 to be notably higher than 2025. This is a tailwind that will help companies like TTMI and others that provide the products that enable the hyperscalers to do what they want to do with AI and data centers. The recent bottlenecks in memory products required in AI and data center applications are a prime example of demand surpassing supply massively, and currently, there may not be a supply shortage for the company’s HDI PCBs, backplanes, and RF components, but that doesn’t mean that this is going to be like that going forward. There was no shortage of memory supply very recently, and look where we are now. If the expansion efforts in Syracuse and Penang take longer than expected, I believe there will be some bottlenecks that will increase pricing for the vital parts to enable hyperscalers to continue to grow at the pace that they want. Memory stocks like Micron (MU), SanDisk (SNDK), and Western Digital (WDC), all of which received either a Buy or a Strong Buy throughout the year, have massively outperformed the market, increasing from 185% to 429% since my articles were published.

We are seeing a similar situation happen to TTMI’s share price, which is up almost 300%. It seems that investors already see that there might be some bottlenecks in the future, which will help it improve its profitability further. Does it mean the growth is already priced in? That is a tough one to answer. I am leaning more towards the idea that the super cycle is going to continue, and the company will see a massive increase in its operations, but also that will mean the company’s future earnings releases have no room for anything other than perfection.

Valuation

Let’s see if it is already priced in with a quick DCF model. Below are the assumptions I went ahead with that reflect the company’s current updated outlook.

For revenues, I went with around the midpoint of what the company said it can achieve (15%-20% top-line growth). For operating margins to give me $4.71 EPS for 2027, which is what Truist has for that year, I went with around 19.4% that increases only slightly over the next 5 years. The tax rate seemed to be too high, so I went with around 23%, and since the company is increasing its capex to meet the demand, I went ahead with a much higher number for the next few years that will stabilize to around 6% in the long run. Also, since PP&E will be higher, so will be the D&A. These assumptions give us the following EPS projections.

For my intrinsic value calculation, I decided to use 10% as my discount rate instead of 11.2% that the company’s current WACC. I find it too high, so that also means there is essentially no margin of safety. With that said, we get the company’s intrinsic value to be right where it currently trades.

If we want to be a bit more conservative and use the company’s WACC as our discount rate, we get around 14% downside from its current valuation.

Closing Comments

So, at these levels, there seems to be very little upside, if any, when looking at the projections and assumptions. It seems like everything is already priced in. Does that mean that it is? Of course not. The sentiment is on these companies’ side right now. Exuberance is through the roof, so I don’t doubt we will see further share price increases going forward. It just means to me that it is not a good time to jump in, especially because I am already trying to diversify some of my holdings away from AI plays, which will no doubt cost me some gains, but I don’t feel comfortable having all my eggs in this basket for now.

So, if you already own TTMI and enjoyed the gains so far, there is still a lot of room to run, and I think you either continue to hold it or trim some of the top and leave the rest running.

data.title: Franklin Biotechnology Discovery Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: Global equities delivered solid gains in 2025’s third quarter, buoyed in part by easing trade tensions and optimism surrounding artificial intelligence (AI). Central bank actions—notably the US Federal Reserve’s policy interest- rate cut and its signaling of potential further monetary easing—supported market sentiment, as did generally robust corporate earnings. In broad terms, health care stocks exhibited improving fundamentals and posted modest gains, but they also underperformed all but two out of 10 other major equity sectors globally (per MSCI index data in US dollar terms). Among the six health care-related industries, overall gains for biotechnology stocks roughly tripled the sector average, though life sciences tools and services companies, along with other technology-enabled segments (health care-focused software, data, digital health, health technology) also fared well. In contrast to biotech’s rally, pharmaceuticals companies lagged the sector average while the health care equipment and supplies industry sold off. Market headwinds came from large pharma—pressured by patent expirations, drug-pricing risks and potential tariff measures—and from weaker results among equipment and supplies companies facing cost and demand challenges.
  • Contributors: Robust support from stock selection in the fund’s core biotechnology and pharmaceuticals holdings.
  • Detractors: Adverse stock selection and underweighting in the life sciences tools and services industry, along with selloffs for some of the fund’s higher-conviction biotech holdings.
  • Outlook: The portfolio continues to be overweighted in mid-, small- and micro-capitalization biotechnology stocks as these market-cap tiers are where we see some of the best value heading into the fourth quarter. We also continue to emphasize selectivity, favoring what we believe to be clinically or commercially “derisked” assets.

Performance Review

  • The fund’s biotech holdings collectively gained more than twice as much as those tracked by the NASDAQ Biotechnology Index. Key contributors included Abivax (ABVX), which posted a stock return of about 1,000%, along with double- or triple-digit percentage stock gains for overweighted/off-benchmark positions in uniQure, Mineralys Therapeutics (MLYS), Merus (MRUS) (sold by period-end), Centessa Pharmaceuticals (CNTA), Insmed, Olema Pharmaceuticals (OLMA) and more than two dozen others. Our biotech results were also supported by a lack of exposure to numerous poor-performing index component companies that sold off.
  • We believe the fund was benefiting from the shift toward non-acute, data analytics and remote care, while specialty pharma/digital services remained as a structural theme. Renewed investor interest in growth areas such as health tech was supported by easing macro pressures, stabilizing inflation expectations and hopes for regulatory moderation. Many biotech companies were benefiting from positive clinical trial data and groundbreaking AI developments in drug discovery, CRISPR (clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats) gene editing, mRNA (messenger ribonucleic acid) therapies, obesity/diabetes specialty drug growth, and personalized medicine.
  • Among the biotech contributors cited above, Insmed was one of our largest positions. Insmed, which specializes in rare and serious lung diseases, received US Food & Drug Administration (FDA) approval of Brinsupri to treat bronchiectasis (an inflammatory lung condition). The drug received a broad label with no unexpected safety warnings or limitations. This is the first-ever approved treatment for non-cystic fibrosis bronchiectasis.
  • The fund would have fared even better versus the Nasdaq Biotechnology Index if not for detrimental stock selection in the life sciences tools and services industry. This is where key detractor Avantor (AVTR) and other off-index holdings sold off, and where the fund’s lack of investment in several rallying index component companies—namely Medpace Holdings (MEDP) and Tempus AI (TEM)— further dampened relative performance. In the pharma industry, mild losses for off-benchmark Eli Lilly (LLY) and a slightly overweight stake in Crinetics Pharmaceuticals (CRNX) (sold by period-end) eroded the fund’s absolute and relative gains.
  • The rest of the key detractors pressured our otherwise solid results in biotech, where overweight or off-benchmark ARS Pharmaceuticals (SPRY), MoonLake Immunotherapeutics (MLTX) (sold by period-end), Soleno Therapeutics (SLNO), Akebia Therapeutics (AKBA) and Cullinan Therapeutics (CGEM) failed to advance. Underweighted positions in Alnylam Pharmaceuticals and BeOne Medicines (ONC) (formerly BeiGene) further curbed our results as they both rose about 40%, though Alnylam was still a major fund holding that added solidly to the fund’s standalone gains.

Outlook

  • Our positive outlook is based on what we consider to be generally strong fundamentals and appealing valuations backed partly by innovation in new drug modalities, what we see as a potentially strong merger-and-acquisition (M&A) cycle returning later this year and in 2026—after sticking points related to tariffs and Most Favored Nation (MFN) pricing policies are resolved—and relatively stable Medicare drug reimbursement. As it pertains to M&A, we are still waiting to see specific implementation details in terms of how the Trump administration works through looming tariff and MFN pricing policy actions. Some headway was made on both fronts in September, as select pharmaceutical firms began to forge detailed agreements (including exemptions) with the US government. We view these as important “clearing events” that can help quell investor uncertainty and potentially open up biotech M&A activity once they’re clarified.
  • Conversely, we remain cognizant of risks to biopharma innovation and stock performance linked to the persistence of inflation and the potential for only minimal interest-rate reductions in the United States for the remainder of 2025. We are also concerned about worse-than-expected impacts from the long-awaited implementation of Inflation Reduction Act (IRA, signed into law in 2022) drug pricing measures in the United States; its new prescription drug price negotiation provision, inflation caps and Medicare Part D redesign hold the potential to compress profit margins and deter innovation. With negotiated prices now public, empirical evidence is starting to test investor fears that have been active for about three years now. That said, we don’t want to overstate the risk because the IRA pertains to US revenues, and only Medicare revenues therein, which means drugs that skew towards older populations.

data.title: Franklin Intermediate Government Bond SMA Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: Market sentiment improved across the third quarter of 2025 (Q3) due to a partial resolution of some of the outstanding trade policy concerns and a rate cut of 25 basis points (bps) by the US Federal Reserve (Fed), which were both well received by the market. The Fed signaled that more cuts were likely to come by the end of this year. This bolstered markets, which viewed that the cut was addressing some of the weakening economic measures, such as the status of the US labor market. Over the period, US Treasury (UST) yields moved lower with the yield curve steepening. The one-month UST bill fell 11 bps, and three-month UST bill yields declined 36 bps. Yields moved lower by 11 bps to 3.61% on the two-year UST note and by six bps to 3.74% on the 1ve-year UST note. By the end of the quarter, the yield on the benchmark 10-year UST note moved eight bps lower to 4.15%.
  • Contributors: Security selection among USTs. Overweight 10-year portion of the UST yield curve.
  • Detractors: Underweight short-duration USTs.
  • Outlook: The path of the fed funds rate is uncertain. The US job market has shown signs of stress with low job creation; however, inflation remains well above the Fed’s target. We are projecting one to two cuts in the remainder of the year. In our view, the Fed will have much room to continue to cut in an aggressive fashion in 2026 due to stubborn inflation impacted by tariff increases. In our view, intermediate- and long-maturity UST yields will move higher over the course of the fourth quarter.

Performance Review

  • Yield curve positioning detracted from returns primarily from an underweight to the two-year portion of the UST yield curve and yields fell in Q3. In contrast, overweight exposure to the 10-year area of the curve contributed to results. Security selection in UST helped returns for the period.

Outlook

  • The Fed has been struggling to balance its dual mandate—maximum employment and price stability—as stagflation (rising inflation with slowing growth) becomes a real possibility. The Fed has chosen to prioritize employment over inflation, consistent with its historical tendency to intervene when growth slows.
  • The labor market remains within the Fed’s target (unemployment at 4.3%), but inflation is still at 3% and showing little signs that it is returning to the central bank’s 2.0% target. With hiring rates low and unemployment drifting up, the Fed now sees employment risks as more pressing than inflation risks, prompting the first rate cut this year and signaling more cuts ahead.
  • US trade policy remains a wild card, adding to uncertainty in the market. Even if tariffs don’t cause a permanent lift in inflation, strong consumer spending and supportive 1scal policy mean the current economic environment is not likely to return to a disinflation level. In our view, tariffs are expected to cause a moderate, one-off burst of inflation, about 1.0-1.5 percentage points. The overall impact should be limited due to the small share of imports in the US economy, but there’s uncertainty about supply chain effects and the risk of prolonged inflation. In our view, it would be very difficult for the Fed to pivot back in the direction of holding the fed funds rate, or potentially increasing it, given the Fed’s movement toward easing financial conditions, even if inflation moves sustainably higher.
  • There is high uncertainty about future policy, even between members of the FOMC, as reflected in the large distribution seen in the Fed’s SEP scattered projections for the path of fed funds. The FOMC was nearly evenly split between those seeing two or more additional cuts by the end of this year (necessary to defend economic growth and the US job market) and those projecting just one cut (members that may have concerns over lingering inflation and tariff worries). In our view, this level of uncertainty has the potential to spur volatility in the US Treasury market.

data.title: Realty Income: A Top Income Pick In 2026 Yielding 5.5%
data.description:
Realty Income Corporation (O) continues to be one of the most sought after equities in the stock market after declaring 667 monthly dividends and providing investors with 113 quarterly increases. Realty Income’s business model utilizes long-term triple net leases for its commercial real estate that scales across different property types.

The reason why I am so bullish on Realty Income heading into 2026 is because of their durable occupancy, contractual rent growth, and a growing dividend that exceeds the risk-free rate of return. I believe that Realty Income looks enticing for investors, prioritizing dependable cash flow with the potential for capital appreciation. The Fed cut 3 times in 2025 and with a new Fed Chair on the horizon I believe that we will see a much looser operating environment for REITs in 2026. Realty Income is in a position where their cost of capital will decline, and they may be able to issue debt in a lower rate to repay higher yielding debt to expand margins.

As the risk-free rate of return declines, I believe that Realty Income will become more sought after, and it could make its way back to its pre-pandemic highs. I am adding to Realty Income as I feel it’s undervalued in today's market.

Following Up On My Previous Article About Realty Income

Back in September, I wrote an article on Realty Income (can be read here) and discussed how their strategic pivot could take shares higher, Realty Income is expanding their global footprint throughout Europe and launching its own private capital fund which was focusing on institutional investors. Realty Income was able to access cheaper rates on debt in the European markets, which allows them to increase spreads, which expands margins and earnings.

I am following up with a new article to start off 2026 to discuss why Realty Income continues to be a top income pick of mine after another dividend increase. Realty Income is now yielding 5.5% and operating in a better operating environment than they have over the past several years. I wouldn’t be surprised if Realty Income can break out in 2026 as rates continues to decline and income investors look for opportunities to exceed the risk-free rate of return in the capital markets.

Q3 Earnings Were Bullish For Realty Income And Additional Momentum Was Gained

In Q3, Realty Income generated $1.39 billion in revenue and $1.07 of funds from operations (FFO) per share. The Q3 results continued to validate why it is a core holding for many income investors, as their portfolio occupancy was 98.7%. Over the past several years due to the rise of e-commerce and the high-interest rate environment, there was a bear thesis growing that revolved around some of Realty Incomes tenants becoming unstable. Quarter after Quarter Realty Income continues to debunk this thesis as their occupancy rate has never fell below 96.6% since 2000. Realty Income’s historical median occupancy level is 98.3% compared to 94.4% for the REITs in the S&P 500 (SP500). Realty Income’s occupancy rate improved QoQ from 98.6^ to 98.7% while having 204 properties available for lease or sale at the end of Q3.

Realty Income has an exceptional footprint that spans 15,542 properties with 349 million square feet of leasable space. One of the main reasons why I am so bullish on Realty Income is because they have 1,647 clients across 92 industries, which provides a level of diversification that continues to show up in the occupancy numbers. Despite any economic cycle, we have lived through over the past several decades, Realty Income’s occupancy rate has allowed the rents to be collected and the dividend to be expanded on an ongoing basis.

Realty Income was very active in Q3, as they invested $1.4 billion at an average cash yield of 7.7% which included deploying $1 billion of capital into the European markets. The European investments had an average cash yield of 8% which is beneficial for forward profitability growth. This should be bullish for Realty Income because the additional net leases should improve the odds that new investments are AFFO accretive. Realty Income continued to utilize their proprietary AI analytics to create actionable data for their asset management team, which enabled them to sell 140 properties for $215 million. The team also accomplished a 103.5% rent recapture rate on re-leased units in Q3 where the new annualized base rent was $70.65 million compared to the previous annual rent of $68.29 million on the same properties.

To some, this may not seem like a big deal, but it’s a bullish data point as it indicates that lease rollovers are not a structural drag on its underlying business and can become a tailwind in the future.

I believe that Realty Income is positioned well for the future as they have multiple growth levers beyond their net leases. Realty Income raised their AFFO guidance for the 2025 fiscal year, which correlates to their investment volume increasing throughout 2025. This should also provide a solid foundation for the 2026 top and bottom line. What I found interesting on the Q3 earnings call was that management said it launched a perpetual life fund that will provide additional capital to support growth objectives and enhance liquidity. The private capital will allow Realty Income to diversify its funding sources and reduce the reliance on public equity issuance. This could also create the ability to create fee-like earnings streams, depending on how this is structured.

There is a lot of momentum heading into 2026, as Realty Income’s rent recapture rate was 103.5% while their occupancy rate came in at 98.7%, which is an indication that the portfolio is holding up well and that lease rollover is not undermining cash flow. Investing $1.4 billion in Q3 at a 7.7% initial cash yield can support multi-year compounding and dividend expansion. Realty Income is guiding that their investment volume will reach $5.5 billion, which should increase their cash flow capacity and growth prospects this year. As rates continue to decline, the large dividend yield which is paid monthly will continue to look more enticing. I am excited to see what Really Income has been able to accomplish in Q4, as they could be level setting for expanded expectations in 2026.

Realty Incomes Dividend Is Exactly What Income Investors Are Looking For, Yielding 5.5%

The reality is that the Fed has taken rates from 5.5% down to 3.75% since September 2024. The risk-free rate of return is declining, and investors are only able to get 3.52% on the United States 12-Month Bill (US12M). The sidelines are no longer as lucrative as they once were and between Kevin Warsh and Kevin Hasset there is an 85% chance that one of them will become the next Fed Chair according to the prediction markets. CME Group is projecting that the most likely scenario is that we see 2 rate cuts in 2026, but I believe that we could see 3 cuts in 2026 and another 3 cuts in 2027 as either Kevin Warsh or Kevin Hasset could have a very dovish effect on the Fed. Once rates fall below 3%, there will be significantly less reason to stay on the sidelines to generate income, and Realty Income could be a magnet for capital.

The reality is that there aren’t many dividend companies like Realty Income. Many companies pay their dividend on a quarterly basis and have a yield that is in the low single digits. Realty Income provides investors with monthly recurring income that has characteristics that are almost impossible to replicate. Realty Income has declared 667 consecutive monthly dividends and increased the dividend on a quarterly basis 113 times. The compound annual growth rate on Realty Income’s dividend is 4.2% since 1994. Today Realty Income pays $3.24 per share from its dividend which is a 5.5% yield. Realty Income has increased the dividend for 32 years and continues to be a standout in the income space.

From a dividend quality perspective, I think that Realty Income is one of the best dividend companies in the market, as its cash flow is backed by real-world assets that companies need to conduct their business. I believe that the dividend will become more attractive as time progresses and rates decline.

Risks To Investing In Realty Income

While I am bullish on Realty Income, there are several risk factors to consider. Investors need to be aware that Realty Income has been an income producing asset and is unlikely to outperform the market. Investing in the SPDR S&P 500 Trust (SPY) has returned 98% over the past 5-years, while Realty Income has returned 34%. One of the biggest mistakes I see is that investors get enticed by the yield and overlook total returns. If unemployment continues to increase, it could cause the U.S. to go into a recession in the future. If this happens, it could cause some of Realty Income’s tenants to reduce their footprints or abruptly end their leases if they can’t maintain their current operations. Realtors could make a larger shift to e-commerce and limit their physical footprint, which would also negatively impact Realty Income.

Everyone should do their own due diligence and be aware of what they are buying, and realize that Realty Income has risks from the macroeconomic economy that could be quite impactful if they come to fruition.

Conclusion

The Fed closed out 2025 by cutting rates 3 times, which has created a better operating environment for Realty Income. I believe that Realty Income is positioned to deliver recurring dividend income that is growing for income investors because it is combining high occupancy levels throughout its portfolio while executing large levels of investments that are yielding close to 8%. Realty Income raised guidance for 2025, and I believe that they will compound into 2026 as more investments are made.

I believe that the market is overlooking the fact that the macroeconomic environment is setting up well for Realty Income as interest rates are declining allowing Realty Income to lower their cost of capital and creating an environment where their tenants will have an easier time accessing the capital markets. I believe that Realty Income is still a top income pick for 2026 and that it can generate capital appreciation while generating large amounts of recurring dividend income.

data.title: Franklin Growth Allocation Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Financial market sentiment improved across the third quarter of 2025. Within the United States, stock markets gained due to increased clarity into US tariff policies, a resilient economy and a more dovish US Federal Reserve (Fed). Early in the quarter, the United States reached trade deals with a number of countries—including the United Kingdom, European Union and Japan—relieving some of the associated uncertainties. Although deals were not reached with other large trading partners, there was an additional 90-day extension in tariff increases with China.
  • Global equity markets also advanced through the third quarter of 2025 as tariff anxieties gradually eased, central banks tilted more accommodative and technology leadership persisted. July’s tone improved on clearer trade signals and resilient US earnings; August added gains despite episodic volatility around new tariff measures; and September capped the quarter with a US Fed rate cut that steadied rate-sensitive assets and lifted risk appetite.
  • The US Treasury (UST) yield curve steepened slightly, as yields fell more sharply at the short end of the curve, in response to elevated expectations for Fed easing. Global aggregate bond indexes posted modest gains in the third quarter as yield changes varied across countries.

Performance Review

  • Over the third quarter, Franklin Growth Allocation Fund (Advisor Class without sales charges) posted a solid gain and modestly outperformed its benchmark.
  • The fund was underweight bonds for the quarter, which was supportive as equities led performance among asset classes. Within fixed income, a US overweight and international underweight benefited relative returns.
  • A slight overweight in equities strengthened relative performance. From our perspective, the macroeconomic backdrop continues to provide support for stock markets, with the Fed’s easing bias and rate cuts occurring alongside resilient growth. Within equity, an international underweight and a US overweight also lifted relative results.
  • Off-benchmark exposure to alternatives contributed to performance as an exchange-traded fund delivered a robust gain.
  • In contrast, fund selection in equity detracted from relative returns, although underlying equity positions collectively delivered gains on an absolute return basis.
  • An underweight allocation to emerging market (EM) equities dampened relative returns as a strong rally in large-cap technology names led Chinese markets higher. EM risk assets were also supported by a weakening US dollar trend, expectations for further Fed rate cuts and momentum following gains in recent months.

Outlook

  • The strength of the current global equity rally has surprised many market participants, considering stocks have brushed off tariff uncertainty, inflation concerns and labor market weakness to post gains close to 30% since a post-Liberation Day nadir in early April. US technology stocks have done even better, with the Nasdaq rising almost 50% in the same period. Such a powerful advance naturally brings some caution.
  • However, we prefer to take a more measured approach, examining the underlying macro and corporate environment. Our analysis finds a broadly positive setup for equities, where improving business activity and solid economic growth have provided the platform for a strong second-quarter earnings season. Forward earnings-per-share growth forecasts for global stocks have risen into double digits, led by US companies, while there is also a clear improvement in the magnitude and breadth of earnings revisions.
  • From a cross-asset perspective, we have balanced our upgraded view of equities by strengthening our pessimism toward fixed income. We believe market expectations for Fed easing are too lofty, given resilient growth and persistent inflation pressures, while fiscal instability could put further upward pressure on long-term yields.

data.title: Equifax Stock Faces Long-Term Headwinds Despite Positive Q4 Outlook
data.description:
Equifax Inc. (EFX) and its credit bureau peers have had a volatile fourth quarter on the news front and on the market. Following Fair Isaac Corporation's (FICO) early-October press release announcing the launch of its direct licensing program as a way of "eliminating reliance on the three nationwide credit bureaus," the stock is down 22 percent.

Regardless, the stock does appear to hold some upward momentum that has resurfaced several times since the beginning of the fourth calendar quarter, which is also EFX's fourth fiscal quarter.

While this could look like recovery in progress after a bad news cycle, the decline narrative for EFX stock could end up having a very long tail. My recommendation is to Sell, because although there are some revenue drivers in the horizon, a prolonged journey to the goal of "8-12% LT revenue growth" does not make a compelling case for holding the stock now.

FICO's Move in Perspective

The direct license program for tri-merge resellers is a contentious topic so I shall avoid commenting on whether it was 'fair' of Fair Isaac Corp to initiate this move or not, but the impact of the announcement was immediate. The very next day after the FICO release, EFX fired its own missive. An excerpt:

FICO’s doubling of their FICO score pricing for mortgage in 2026 and the announcement of their Mortgage Direct License Program is another example of FICO flexing its monopoly pricing power. The company’s announcement was inaccurately positioned as a cost savings, when it will actually end up costing consumers and the mortgage industry much more. FICO has more than doubled its previously disclosed pricing for 2026, raising prices from $4.95 to $10. This monopoly-like 2x price increase has the potential to raise mortgage score costs across the industry by approximately $100 million.

This is a continuation of FICO’s established pattern of aggressive pricing actions from their historical sole source position in the Mortgage space – with pricing increases made at a CAGR of 150% over the past 4 years.

This case of classic defensive posturing was not entirely unforeseen. Over 2025, the three credit unions came out with a barrage of actions, ranging from product bundling (VantageScore 4.0 free for FICO score purchasers through 2026, for example), to adding rent and utility payment history to VantageScore, to outright accusations in the quote above, and so on.

The bottom line for investors is that the three credit bureau stocks immediately sank on FICO's news shocker, and three months later, there is still a 20 percent performance delta between FICO and EFX. The latter was the worst hit of the three credit bureaus, but seems to have regained some of its upward momentum in the past week and is currently 10 percent down over the past one quarter and a bit.

Analysts at Jeffries initially estimated that "The new model could hit credit bureau earnings by an average of 10% to 15%," so it would seem that EFX is now down by a commensurate amount.

On the surface, it would seem that this beatdown is completely unjustified. After all, the Q3 conference call brought with it a full year guidance raise on both lines, with Q4 revenue now expected to be $40 million higher and adjusted earnings to be 12 cents higher. At the upper end of guidance, investors can expect a top line print of $1.536 billion and $2.08 in adjusted EPS.

To me, this does not appear to be a business in decline. With Equifax Cloud now in place, we should see a lower impact at the bottom line than at the top. The fight against FICO is an ongoing one, and EFX already has a strategy in place to mitigate any losses on this front. The bundling through 2026 and the intent to keep the price stable through 2027 are both supportive of such a strategy, and with additional leverage now coming from cloud deployment, I do not see that big of a problem posed by FICO's move.

However, EFX poses a different kind of problem; one in which the superior performance of its peer group is the very cause of its stock price woes. We shall get into that later, but one should first look at where this growth is coming from, and more importantly, is it enough for the market? That is where any bullish thesis would hit a snag.

Where Can One Expect Further Growth to Come From?

Focusing on Q4 and beyond, we see Street analysts now being in line with management's optimistic take. Midpoint revenue is guided at $1.52 billion, while the consensus stands at $1.53 billion. Although analysts have been bearish on Q4 EPS, with 15 of 18 revisions being downward, they appear to be bullish for FY 2026, with 18 of 20 analysts revising EPS upwards in the last 90 days.

EFX has beaten on both lines for the past three quarters, and on earnings for the past eight quarters, which informs me that management's guidance is typically conservative, particularly with respect to earnings. A guidance midpoint of $2.03 against an analyst consensus of $2.05 might not seem like a significant delta, but I suspect we shall see a beat on Q4 adjusted EPS. We are still nearly a month away from the next release, but I think the consensus is locked in for now.

As for what will drive that growth, we should go beyond the rosy picture management has painted. What is not made explicit in the company filings, reports, and presentations is often as important as what is.

Management's Take Vs. My Take

The company has been very transparent about 7 percent decline it has modeled as the downside for hard pull credit, but it has guided for 26 percent revenue growth in that part of the USIS segment nonetheless. Implicit in that is the FICO move being completely offset, and then some. I think management has done an excellent job of outlining the mitigatory action to pre-empt the losses from the direct licensing by FICO, but not quite around highlighting the defensive impact it will have on overall growth in the mortgage bucket of its USIS segment. More importantly, the unspoken indication that the increased guidance still fails to match up to industry levels.

I believe that is why the market seems to still struggle with EFX's valuation, because what management has not made explicit, obviously, is that it is the slowest-growing of the three credit agencies despite the optimistic guidance.

The lens I am looking through shows me an upbeat management whose story the market is simply not buying at this point. The story might be legitimate, but in the context of what makes a good investment, its peers are largely outshining it at the top and bottom.

So, on the one side, that there are definitely some growth drivers is an established fact. This encapsulates the core bull case. All three segments are expected to growth at mid to high single digit percentages come Q4/FY earnings. Management has made quite a bit of noise around addressing the FICO move, but on the other side, investors are not being distracted from the fact that these growth rates hardly justify the valuation premium that EFX carries within this peer group.

As one can clearly see, EFX's operating income multiple is the richest of the three credit bureaus but there is also quite a bit of downward pressure. One can also see this is the vast chasm between TTM and forward price to sales and price to earnings multiples, particularly on a YTD basis.

The market is not setting any high benchmarks for EFX, that is what I see. It also does not seem to help that management is guiding for a revenue growth range of 8 to 12 percent, long-term, under the "New EFX" war cry. I suspect the market sees this as a long-shot. Not entirely improbable, especially with cloud capabilities going online and AI assisting with the heavy lifting as we enter the first fiscal quarter of 2026, but not very likely, either, until we see the top line approach these targets in a tangible way.

In a situation like that, I would not want to be associated with the stock. I have not assessed the peers in similar detail, but I already suspect there are better opportunities within this niche.

In that vein, I cannot recommend a Hold, let alone a Buy. My call on EFX is a Sell, and to be perfectly clear, it is not the direction of the business and its metrics that I am worried about as much as the fact that it is sorely lagging behind its peers who, more or less, offer similar credit solutions. I know they are vastly different businesses, but their overlapping lines of service put them in direct competition with each other on several fronts, making this a reasonable comparison.

For now, EFX is a Sell - until we see a meaningful bump in revenue growth; in particular, investors should look at positive movements in the Workforce Solutions segment, which is operationally the most profitable of the three units.

Going even deeper, one should watch the impact of tightened verification requirements after the enactment of OB3, as this could be a strong revenue driver. A 7 percent growth rate was reported in Q3 for non-mortgage verifier revenues, and a lot of that is going to come from verifications in consumer lending.

My Sell recommendation is not likely to be short-lived, because the key indicators still have a long way to go in supporting a realistic double-digit topline growth rate. However, if the tide begins to turn in a meaningful way, I might consider a more neutral view of EFX. For now, my opinion is that the opportunity cost of staying invested in EFX is a lot higher than the eventual payoff.

data.title: Planet Labs Needs $1 Billion In Sales To Drive Upside
data.description:
When thinking about aerospace and defense, many investors think about missiles, fighters, and, more recently, drones. However, a critical part of defense is intelligence, and that is where, among other elements, imagery comes into play. One way to gather intelligence through imagery is by using satellites, and that is where Planet Labs (PL) comes into play. In this report, I discuss Planet Labs’ Q3 2026 earnings and discuss why I believe the stock is too expensive at this point.

Record Revenue For Planet Labs

Revenue climbed $20 million to $81.3 million, while the company is generating positive EBITDA at $5.6 million compared to slightly loss-making in the comparable quarter last year. The Q3 2026 earnings mark the fourth consecutive quarter with positive EBITDA. We note that defense and intelligence revenues grew more than 70% year-on-year, driven by data subscription services as well as satellite services. Civil government sales were up 1%, with growth constrained by downsizing of the budget for NASA and the U.S. government shutdown. Commercial revenues are currently down, but we note that this is partially driven by Planet Labs’ focus on large government contracts. By region, we see that North America grew 30%, but the higher growth is achieved in the Asia Pacific region and Europe, with 38% growth, as European countries are increasingly looking for sovereign intelligence solutions, and in that effort, they also contract with U.S.-based commercial parties such as Planet Labs.

Defense Is The Growth Vectors For Planet Labs

The core of Planet Labs’ business model has been selling earth imagery for which the company charges a recurring fee. Moreover, the company offers analytics platforms, which basically can be used to “make sense” of the imagery. Artificial intelligence is one of the items that the company can increasingly rely on to drive analytics capabilities. In some sense, everything that has to do with security, whether it is energy security, food security, or protection of countries or assets, belongs to the addressable market of Planet Labs. So, the addressable market is large. The company is also pivoting more to satellite manufacturing, opening up additional revenue streams beyond imagery and analytics subscriptions.

The company has a backlog of $734 million, a huge increase compared to $232 million a year ago. Around 61% of the total revenues are currently derived from defense and intelligence customers. As a reference, a year ago the same end market represented 47% of the sales. Planet Labs stock surged following the announcement of a 9-figure multiyear agreement with the Swedish Armed Forces, and I believe that it is one of the agreements that clearly puts on display the demand and contract opportunities for Planet Labs in the defense & intelligence market.

What I like about Planet Labs is that it does everything from imagery to analytics to satellites, making it a vertically integrated company with a diverse customer base with multiple revenue streams. My expectation is that while subscriptions currently are the core of the business, demand for sovereign intelligence solutions will drive satellite sales and satellite-as-a-service sales, which include hardware delivery and multi-year revenues.

Planet Labs Targets >20% Sales Growth

For the fourth quarter, Planet Lab is targeting sales between $76 million and $80 million with gross margins between 50% and 52%. Adjusted EBITDA is expected to be a loss between $5 million and $7 million. This is driven by the company’s investment in next-generation satellite fleet capacity and AI-enabled solutions development. For the full year, the company expects around $300 million in sales with margins of 57%-58% and adjusted EBITDA of $6-$8 million.

Current Valuation Does Not Justify A Bullish View

I have used analyst estimates on sales as well as my own growth assumptions for 2029 and 2030 to run an EV/sales valuation, and while I believe that Planet Labs has a nice business model, we don’t see any upside. I believe that the current stock price fully values the stock and then some through 2030, and that means that from a fundamental perspective, there is no attractive entry point. The current valuation would require the company to generate around $1 billion by 2030 to drive attractive upside.

I am, however, assigning a “Hold” rating, and you could also consider that to be a speculative “Buy” rating, as it could be the case that the inflow of new contracts and additions to the backlog may drive share prices higher.

Conclusion: Planet Labs Is A Promising Company, But Too Expensive

I believe that Planet Labs has strong growth opportunities driven by strong demand in the defense & intelligence end market. This should drive EBITDA and cash flow performance. However, at the same time, we do note that at current prices there is little fundamental support for additional share price appreciation. This makes the stock more of a speculative “Buy” or “Hold” on positive stock price movement from new contract awards.

data.title: Franklin California High Yield Municipal Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: In the third quarter of 2025 (Q3), as per Barclays, new issuance of municipal (muni) bonds exceeded the average seen in the past few years, driven by a 34% year-over-year surge in July issuance. By quarter end, the yield on the benchmark 10-year US Treasury (UST) note fell by eight basis points (bps) to 4.15%, while the 30-year UST yield decreased by four bps to 4.73%. US markets rose on clearer tariff policies, economic resilience, and a more dovish Fed, which cut rates by 25 bps to 4.00%-4.25% in September.
  • Contributors: Overweight allocation to muni bonds with 20 or more years to maturity, overweight allocation to muni bonds rated below investment grade and security selection in BBB rated bonds.
  • Detractors: Overweight allocation to muni bonds with no external credit rating, security selection in A rated bonds and underweight allocation to muni bonds with two to 10 years to maturity.
  • Outlook: Municipal bond valuations continue to appear attractive, particularly on a tax-adjusted basis. In a more challenging environment, strong bottom-up research and disciplined security selection will be essential to identifying relative value and preserving portfolio quality.

Performance Review

  • Over the quarter, Franklin California High Yield Municipal Fund (FVCAX) outperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg Municipal Bond California Exempt Index.
  • The fund’s yield curve positioning contributed to relative performance during the third quarter, primarily driven by our overweight to muni bonds with 20 or more years to maturity. However, our underweight to muni bonds with two to 10 years to maturity curbed returns.
  • Rating allocations detracted from relative returns for the period, owing to our overweight to bonds with no external credit rating. Conversely, our overweight to bonds rated below investment grade partially lifted results. Security selection benefited relative results, particularly due to selection in BBB rated securities. Meanwhile selection in A rated securities hindered results.

Outlook

  • Muni bond issuance exceeded historical averages in Q3 as issuers, who had been reluctant to issue new bonds under the looming threat to their tax-exempt status, came to market. Positive fund flows into the asset class were not able to keep up with the supply. The muni bond yield curve continues to steepen, with longer-maturity bond valuations cheapening somewhat.
  • Fundamentals remain broadly stable, however, as the economy slows and inflationary pressures ease, tax revenue growth at the state and local level is expected to moderate, placing greater importance on credit selectivity going forward.
  • Municipal bond valuations continue to appear attractive, particularly on a tax-adjusted basis. Yields are near multi-year highs, allowing investors to lock in compelling income. In a more challenging environment, strong bottom-up research and disciplined security selection will be essential to identifying relative value and preserving portfolio quality.

data.title: Gold Miners Look Expensive After Strong 2025 Performance
data.description:
Introduction

Gold had its best year in 2025 (+67.4%), since 1979 (+118.4%). That is great for this gold bug, who has been analyzing and investing in gold and gold mining companies since my days with Marketocracy. I have always kept a position in gold in my allocation and always will. Since January is my traditional rebalancing month, I trimmed my gold positions and my gold mining positions. It is there, I begin to tell you that gold mining’s froth is spilling over the brim, and it is time to sell some of your holdings.

Methodology

Unfortunately, S&P Global, which owns the Dow Jones subindices, does not publish publicly available fact sheets for the DJ Gold Miners Index. I do know from secondary sources that Newmont Corporation (NYSE: NEM) is its most significant component. Given that the index is market-cap weighted and is limited to those companies listed in U.S. Markets, I am not surprised. It does appear from the S&P rulebook that there must be sufficient liquidity.

Gold Mining Performance

Using data from the Dow Jones US Gold Mining Index, gold mining finished 2025 at an all-time high, up 176.6% for the year. My data show that this is not normal behavior for the index and, frankly, could cause significant recency bias in the sector. What the data do not show, but the chart does, is that the index TOTALED a return of only 44.8% from 1992 through 2024.

This is an ugly table, and the chart is even more hideous. Let us see why.

The Good (Diversification)

The gold mining sector does offer diversification for one’s portfolio. Its historical beta (0.41) indicates decoupling from the S&P 500, and its R2 (0.03) indicates no correlation with the overall market.

The Bad (Risk Efficiency & Downside Protection)

In addition to the beta, the index does offer some protection during down markets (0.50% v. -15.27%), but it has a nasty tail risk (-61.57%). For my three favorite risk measures, the gold mining index does not cut it. Compared to the S&P 500, it can deliver performance against the risk-free rate (0.22 v. 0.60), inflation (0.22 v. 0.60), and predictable positive returns (0.11 v. 0.69). I cannot imagine anyone believing that gold mining offers an attractive risk profile. It gets worse.

The Ugly (The Return Profile)

Do not be fooled by what happened in 2025. This index hit an all-time high in May 1996 and did not establish a new high until October 2011. When one looks at the performance history, this index suffers from troughs that last for decades before one can even think about recovering their original investment. That explains why its overall performance pales in comparison to the S&P 500 (4.16% v. 10.69%). The data shows that it does not even fully participate in up markets (8.78% v. 17.73%). Finally, one can see that it does not establish a sound methodology (α = -1.72%). Consequently, it does not even generate a respectable return after adjusting for risk (M2 = 4.17% v. 10.20%).

Investing in Gold Mining Is Not Investing in Gold

Investing in a gold mining company is different from investing in gold. One can see that when the mining index and gold are decoupled (β = 0.29). When they study the coefficient of determination (R2 = 0.48), it is clear that investing in gold mining is not investing in gold. So why is that?

Mining companies are businesses. They have statements of income, balance sheets, and statements of cash flow. They must manage their debt, generate a profit, and yield positive cash flows just like any other company. The former CEO of Randgold Resources, Mark Bristow, once said, “The industry blows its brains out every time. The reason we are still in the industry is because the competition isn’t that sharp.” It was such a shame when Barrick Gold bought out his company, in my opinion. His general take was that gold mining companies could not manage their debt. Ironically, he became the CEO of Barrick.

Action Items

First, fall in love with gold without the miners. That said, I had remarkable success with mining companies when I was a member of the Marketocracy 100. I looked for companies that managed their debt and generated positive cash flow. I then looked for companies whose all-in sustainable costs (AISC) were less than the market rate for gold.

All these companies have clean income statements, strong balance sheets, and positive cash flow. Also, their respective AISCs are all below the current spot price for gold. Disclosure: I own AEM. Nevertheless, some of these companies have frothy multiples, so I would probably look at these first. The rest of them have valuations greater than 20. Here are their P/E ratios (TTM):
